The power of the chief priests had steadily increased under the reign of numerous weak monarchs, until the government of the country was effectively in their control. They were governors of cities, commanders on the battlefield, physicians, architects, and scribes. Often, they were seen in secular employment, but only they could enter the temple's sacred precincts and perform its rituals.

The power of the 25th or 'Ethiopian' dynasty was gradually increasing and consolidating itself. Assur-bani-pal, who had conquered Egypt and devastated Thebes, was still reigning at Nineveh. It must have been particularly humiliating for him to be unable to make another attempt to regain what he had lost. Psammetichus successfully achieved the other portion of his task. He reunited the north under his rule and made peace with the rulers of the south. The sun of her former greatness had indeed set, but under Psammetichus and his successors, she enjoyed a long and brilliant afterglow of light.

Psammetichus' long reign (c. 664 - 610 BCE) was marked by a single significant military achievement: the capture of Azotus, which had been under siege for nearly three decades.
